raw.headline: UC Berkeley Law bans AI in all credited coursework effective Summer 2026, diverging sharply from Berkeley's campus-wide GenAI rollout and the system's equivocal detection posture.
raw.what_happened: Effective Summer 2026, UC Berkeley School of Law adopted a policy banning student AI use for conceptualizing, outlining, drafting, revising, translating, or editing any work submitted for credit, and barring all AI in exams. The policy was covered in Forbes on May 22 and Reuters on May 26, and the law school's official policy page was published May 26, 2026. This sits alongside a concurrent June 3, 2025 Berkeley campus memo rolling out Google Gemini App and Zoom AI Companion to all faculty and staff, with student access planned for fall 2025.
raw.why_it_matters: The systemwide Academic Senate transmitted GenAI-specific academic integrity guidance in September 2025, but the baseline records Berkeley's AI detection posture as equivocal — guidance discusses detection tools without endorsing or prohibiting them. Berkeley Law's blanket prohibition represents the sharpest campus-level restriction in the UC system, directly contradicting the concurrent campus-wide Gemini rollout from the same institution. A UC Berkeley CSHE working paper published May 13 found a 13-percentage-point rise in A grades in AI-exposed courses at a large research university, providing empirical pressure behind the law school's move. UC has no systemwide policy that would harmonize or bound these diverging school-level approaches.
raw.for_the_committee: Should the committee recommend that UCOP establish a systemwide floor — or ceiling — for school-level AI restrictions in credited coursework, or is divergence across professional schools an acceptable expression of academic freedom that the system should affirmatively protect?

experts.why: Co-author of 'AI and the University as a Service' (PMLA 2024); UCSB Academic Senate Chair; direct expertise on academic freedom and AI governance in university settings
AVP for UC Online; co-chaired Risk and Transparency subcommittee on prior AI Council; operational view of how AI restrictions land in courses systemwide
Published Forbes piece this week arguing refusal to engage AI is a losing strategy; directly engaged in assessment and academic integrity questions

items.headline: 2,100 UC IT workers unionize with UPTE-CWA, explicitly naming AI deployment and job security as organizing drivers — creating a new collective-bargaining dimension for UC AI governance.
reasons: feed_source.member_id "topic" is not a known committee member

raw.headline: 2,100 UC IT workers unionize with UPTE-CWA, explicitly naming AI deployment and job security as organizing drivers — creating a new collective-bargaining dimension for UC AI governance.
raw.what_happened: On or around May 28, 2026, 2,100 UC information technology professionals voted to join UPTE-CWA, forming the largest tech-worker union in the United States with 8,400 total members. CWA and UPTE both confirmed that AI deployment and job-security concerns were primary organizing drivers. The Berkeley-side reporting on May 21 noted union activists framed representation as a way for workers who best understand AI to shape the university's use of the technology through collective bargaining.
raw.why_it_matters: UC has a systemwide AI governance structure — the AI Council, the Steering Committee, and campus-level bodies — but the baseline records no mechanism for structured workforce input into AI deployment decisions. The OGC AI Alert and Risk Assessment Guide address procurement and deployment risk from a compliance standpoint, but neither establishes a labor-consultation process. The IT workers now represented by UPTE are the same staff who build, maintain, and operate campus AI platforms including TritonGPT, ZotGPT, River, and AggieAI — meaning collective bargaining over AI deployment terms is now a foreseeable governance channel that does not yet appear in any UC AI policy document.
raw.for_the_committee: Should the committee recommend that UCOP proactively establish a labor-consultation framework for AI deployment decisions ahead of contract negotiations with UPTE, or treat AI governance as a reserved management right — and what are the strategic implications of each posture?

feed_sources.title: UC tech workers vote to unionize with desire to shape AI policy

experts.why: UCOP CIO; operational authority over IT workforce and AI platform deployments directly affected by UPTE bargaining unit
UC Academic Senate Chair; deep shared-governance experience; attuned to labor-governance intersections across the system
Student committee member with stated expertise in digital sovereignty and the impact of private vs. public partnerships; workforce equity perspective
